lesson_content.title: Content: 1.1 About this Module
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

Module 1: Writing Basics

Paragraph:

In this module you will be introduced to Keys to Early Writing, a writing instruction framework for evidence-based instruction for the components of beginning writing instruction. This module is aligned to Chapter 1 in the training book.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.1 About this Module
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

This module covers these topics:

  • connections to models of writing instruction (The Writing Rope, The Not So Simple View of Writing) 
  • research about effective beginning writing instruction
  • teaching principles for writing, including the Gradual Release of Responsibility model
  • the difference between composing and transcription skills 
  • overview of spelling and handwriting instruction

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.1 About this Module
lesson_content.content: Paragraph:

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.1 About this Module
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

What is Keys to Early Writing?

Paragraph:

Keys to Early Writing presents a framework for evidence-based writing instruction in kindergarten through grade 2.  It is not a writing program. Rather, it provides the foundational knowledge teachers need to teach all components of beginning writing skills to young students regardless of the published writing program used in the schools where they teach. The instructional topics covered in this course, shown in the graphic above, address all the components that should be present in an early writing curriculum.  A brief description is provided below for each topic.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.1 About this Module
lesson_content.content:
Title: Writing Basics: Description:

instructional framework; early writing development; teaching principles; spelling and handwriting

Title: Writing Community: Description:

writing environment; oral language as a foundation; daily opportunities to write; writing lesson routine

Title: The Writing Process: Description:

think and plan, write, and revise stages; collaboration, conferencing and feedback

Title: Writer's Craft and Text Structure: Description:

using mentor text; text structure for the three types of writing (informational, opinion, narrative)

Title: Drawing, Labels, Lists: Description:

drawing as first steps to writing; oral rehearsal and dictation; lists and labels; linking to spelling and handwriting

Title: Syntax, Sentences: Description:

sentence parts; four types of sentences; activities for developing syntactic awareness

Title: Paragraph Writing: Description:

paragraph structure to support comprehension and writing; paragraph scaffolds; patterns of organization and transitions

Title: Writing from Sources: Description:

using prompts to write about content; shared research and writing

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.1 About this Module
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

A Complete Writing Curriculum Integrates All Components

Paragraph:

Each course module explores one writing component and provides instructional suggestions. However, it is important to remember that a complete writing curriculum for kindergarten through grade 2 should integrate multiple components. For example, during a drawing and labeling lesson in kindergarten, or a paragraph writing lesson in grade 2, the teacher will integrate instructional suggestions from Writing Community, The Writing Process, and Writer's Craft & Text Structure.

Transcript: All of us learn to write by being taught certain skills and strategies but we also learn to write by emulating other writers very simple example if you've been in a car accident and you go down to the police station and you have to write an accident report and you've never written one before what do you do you say can I view some accident reports and see how others do it and then you look at a few models and you go OK I know how to do this right or let's say you're an educator and you're writing a grant for some funding and you've never written a grant before what do you do you look at how other grants have been written and then all of a sudden you emulate it so this is true with young children as well if we can look at how others write really beautiful sentences elaborated sentences if we can see how others use a really great transition word to make a connection between one sentence and another if we're trying to learn how to write an introduction to an opinion or argument piece and how to state a claim and we can see how other people have stated claims we emulate that and that's the whole oh goal or usefulness of what's called mentor text now too often what teachers do with mentor text is they'll take maybe a whole poem or a whole chapter in a book or an article and they'll say this is what a good informational piece will look like so make your writing look like that that's too overwhelming that's the mistake they make the other mistake is that just by showing students a good example of a piece of writing the students will magically figure out how to emulate it what we have to be careful with mentor text is #1 pick the text for a very specific purpose so I want to teach students how to use the trans the transcription word next or finally find a couple of examples they might just be two or three sentences where somebody's really done a great job of that unpack it with the students and then say now you try to do the same thing but with something you're writing about and it's that explicitness the guided practice and that sort of very narrow focus for your mentor text the other thing I'd suggest about mentor text is show more than one example so you know if you can find two or three examples that's even better

Transcript: Writing is a fundamental part of engaging in professional social community and civic activities many employees have at least some responsibility for writing and the ability to write well is a critical component of being able to communicate effectively to a variety of audiences writing is not only a valuable tool for communicating to others but it can also be used to help clarify one's own ideas in the learning process and as a means of self-expression in addition people with strong writing skills may have an advantage throughout their school career and in the job market the authors of the Institute of Education sciences practice guide teaching elementary school students to be effective writers believe that students who develop strong writing skills at an early age acquire valuable tools for learning communication and self-expression these skills can be developed through effective writing instruction practices that include adequate time for students to write this video describes 6 strategies for teaching writing to elementary students based on the recommendations in the practice guide these strategies are also highlighted on the rel SE infographic instructional strategies for teaching writing to elementary students from the time students begin writing in kindergarten it is important that they are provided with explicit instruction not only do students need to learn to physically write letters words and sentences but they need to gain an understanding of the writing process the means through which a writer composes text components of the writing process include planning drafting sharing evaluating revising and editing students must be taught how to engage in each of these and the fact that writing is not a linear process they will often move back and forth amongst components teachers will need to engage in the I do we do you do process to help students learn how to develop their writing skills as students learn to compose text teachers need to provide a variety of strategies to help them engage in the writing process for instance teaching students to organize their thoughts and ideas in an outline before they begin writing may be helpful also using acronyms such as pow for pick ideas organized notes write and say more may help students recall strategies that may help them as they plan draft and finalize writing projects it is important to note that explicit writing instruction should occur across content areas students need to learn how to write depending on their purpose and audience a report for science is written differently than a narrative report written for social studies they will need to learn the important characteristics of different types of writing and should have opportunities over the course of the school year to write for a variety of purposes and audiences mentor texts can be an important tool to help teachers show students what various types of writing look like and to demonstrate the techniques used that are specific to a particular type of text elementary school teachers are the best writers in their classrooms and it is important that they show their students how to write a document camera can be an excellent tool to enable teachers to write in front of their students teachers may think aloud as they demonstrate the various components of writing or engage in writing a specific portion of text such as an introduction students need to see their teachers right think rethink and rewrite more than likely students will not all be at the same level when it comes to the development of their writing skills and they will need varying levels of support teachers will need to identify the strengths and weaknesses in student writing so instruction can be differentiated and scaffolded when necessary when students strengths and needs are identified teachers may work with small groups who need help with a similar skill or perhaps one-on-one with particular students teachers should build on the strengths of their students and provide opportunities for students who are doing well to continue to develop their skills students need not only to develop writing skills but they also need to be motivated to write oftentimes writing centers provide a supportive and collaborative environment so that students are encouraged to grow as writers students may be provided the opportunity to choose their own writing topics work with one another and also share final products in addition a variety of tools may be provided in the centre depending on the grade level of students younger students may have access to paper variety of colors and markers or crayons while older students may use a computer sticky notes highlighters etcetera when students have choices and feel connected to their teachers and to one another they may feel safe to engage freely in the writing process the importance of the ability to write well cannot be overstated and these skills must be taught early when teachers provide explicit instruction teach students to use a variety of strategies provide writing instruction across content areas model the writing process for students provide scaffolding instruction and allow students to collaborate in writing centers students can develop strong writing skills these skills will be invaluable to them as they move through their school careers into their professional lives and engage in civic and community responsibilities

Transcript: The writing rope basically has five pieces to it each one could be represented as if they were strands in a rope right and each one we can talk about it instruction about each level of the writing rope and think about what we need to teach it and to practice it but in the end what students have to be able to do is integrate all of them at the same time like strands in a row and then put them all together to be an effective writing So what people often ask me what what are the strands in the rope what are those components well why did you organize it the way you did the first strand is the critical thinking and I put that up at the top and this involves all the skills and strategies that are needed to be able to write about what you're thinking write about what you're learning write about what you're reading so this includes everything from if you're reading something or watching a video right and you want to write about it it includes that ability to 1st decide what's important and that involves a lot of critical thinking it then involves being able to take some notes somehow to get that down because you're not going to hold it all in your head right so how do you get that down so it's note taking skills but then it's also thinking about how you want to plan what you want to say about what you've just read or learned and that starts to get us into another part of that first strand which is the pages of the writing process that first you have to think a little then you have to plan a little then you write a little then you revise what you wrote and then you recycle through that so that's the critical thinking part of the strand it encompasses an awful lot of skills and strategies as well as the stages in the writing process the second strand in the rope has to do with sentences I call it syntax but it's syntactic awareness actually that you're trying to develop with students and you know if you think about it the individual sentence is like aside from words it's the like the smallest unit of structure in a piece of writing but if you can't write a good sentence you're not going to be able to write anything else that's longer and so it really is the core and then we have a lot of older students who have difficulty with writing and the problem is going right back to that basic sentence level so that's the second strand the third strand of the writing rope has to do with text structure and this this can cover really broad levels of text structure so for example if you think about the three major types or genres of writing the narrative informational and opinion or argument if you're an older student what are the structures that represent those three types how do you write an introduction for each of them or a conclusion how is the body developed you know narrative the organizational structure might be beginning middle end informational it's gonna be big ideas and sub ideas opinion it's gonna be reasons and evidence and counterclaims and rebuttals so that's the broadest level of text structure but the next level down is paragraphs structure and paragraph structure along with sentence writing is are the two sort of basic building blocks of being able to write any piece of text and we have many many students who do not have a sense of what a paragraph is how you can organize your ideas into paragraphs that when you move from one main idea to another main idea that that's time to start a

And then another piece that's very much connected to that are what's called patterns of organization so that includes descriptive writing sequence or chronology cause and effect right problem and solution so patterns of organization and that could be at a particular paragraph level I'm going to describe in this paragraph and then this one I'm going to tell the order or it could be several within one paragraph and then the final piece of the text structure strand of the writing rope our transition words and phrases these are little they're gems they're like worth their weight in gold and students who have good language skills transitions come naturally to them they know to say however and therefore and 1st and next and on the other hand but for students who struggle with language and and and getting their ideas out these words don't come naturally so we need to explicitly teach them and they very often signal a pattern of organization or they they signal that you're about to move from one paragraph to another so that's the third strand of the rope the 4th strand of the writing rope has to do with what I labeled writing craft so this can include anything from let's say how you use dialogue in a narrative to get across a certain emotion right or to get across some ideas that you want the reader to understand it includes all those other literary devices that are out there right you know flashback allegory simile metaphor there's so many of them but the other thing that I tucked in under this strand of the rope is what's referred to often as tap and that's audience and purpose because as a writer if you're not thinking about your audience then you're not your writing isn't going to address the needs of that audience and so a writer needs to always be thinking about who's going to read this what is my purpose for writing this and what is the actual task and that then ends up connecting with a lot of writers craft or writers move now one of the things about writing craft that 4th strand of the rope is that one of the ways to help students learn a particular technique maybe it's how to use a transition word or how to use dialogue between 2 characters is to show students what's called mentor models of that so you pick two or three really good examples of that specific skill that you want to explicitly teach you have the students see how another author has done that you talk about it you think it out loud the teacher actually models that writers move or writers craft or even a writing skill and then the students try it on their own with guided practice the 5th strand in the writing rope is about transcription skills So what does that mean they are literally skills including spelling handwriting and or keyboarding and they're the skills that you need to literally transcribe the words on the page very often these I sort of see these skills as best taught like in a phonics lesson rather than in a lesson where you're teaching students the skills that are associated with the other 4 strands of the rope the critical thinking the text structure the sentence skills the writing craft right because transcription skills are all about with this as it relates to the spelling if students have a word they want to express they need to know how to spell it which means they need to know the alphabetic principle and they need to know how phonemes the sounds and the words are represented by graphemes and all of that is best taught during a phonics lesson where you're learning those skills not only to spell the words but also to decode them I also think especially with young children handwriting actually begins when you're first learning the letter shape associated with the letter sound and so as students are learning a letter sound combination maybe the letter H that goes with the sound if they are also writing that out both the uppercase and the lowercase if they're writing that out as they're saying the sound and seeing the letter it not only supports their growth of alphabetic knowledge but it also that's where they begin beginning handwriting skills one of the thing that I'd like to say about transcription skills I think it has sort of a corollary to something that we see when it comes to reading and that is we know when it comes to decoding skills students as they get older who have not developed solid fluency right that ability to see words sound them out quickly get to the word and read it right we know that if students are not fluent with that by the time they hit grade end of grade 3 or 4 it's going to put it's going to tax the thinking part of their reading their ability to focus on making meaning when they read and I think it's very similar with writing especially by the time students are in 4th grade if they aren't fluent with being able to spell the words they want to say and handwrite or type them out then they're putting so much energy into that transcription part of the act that there's not much left over for the critical thinking or the application of text structure knowledge or how to apply things like being aware of the task audience and purpose and getting across the actual meaning in what you want to say

combination maybe the letter H that goes with the sound if they are also writing that out both the uppercase and the lowercase if they're writing that out as they're saying the sound and seeing the letter it not only supports their growth of alphabetic knowledge but it also that's where they begin beginning handwriting skills one of the thing that I'd like to say about transcription skills I think it has sort of a corollary to something that we see when it comes to reading and that is we know when it comes to decoding skills students as they get older who have not developed solid fluency right that ability to see words sound them out quickly get to the word and read it right we know that if students are not fluent with that by the time they hit grade end of grade 3 or 4 it's going to put it's going to tax the thinking part of their reading their ability to focus on making meaning when they read and I think it's very similar with writing especially by the time students are in 4th grade if they aren't fluent with being able to spell the words they want to say and handwrite or type them out then they're putting so much energy into that transcription part of the act that there's not much left over for the critical thinking or the application of text structure knowledge or how to apply things like being aware of the task audience and purpose and getting across the actual meaning in what you want to say

transcript_content.title: Transcript: 1.1 About this Module - Video Transcript: Video IES Transcription Skills
transcript_content.content: Welcome to the overview on teaching foundational skills to help students become effective writers students need to learn foundational writing skills so that they can focus more on developing and communicating ideas in their writing handwriting spelling sentence construction typing and word processing should become effortless as students move to middle and upper elementary school younger students need explicit instruction and frequent practice to acquire and Polish these skills young writers in kindergarten and 1st grade need to learn how to hold a pencil and form letters correctly teachers should demonstrate how to hold a pencil comfortably between the thumb and forefinger while resting on the middle finger although children may change their grip overtime a comfortable pencil grip will prevent students from getting tired which can discourage writing teachers need to show young children how to form print and cursive letters correctly from memory handwriting practice diagrams can be helpful in showing students how to form letters practicing specific letters in isolation is not sufficient to achieve handwriting proficiency students need to apply their handwriting skills to authentic writing activities handwriting practice is most effective when done in multiple short sessions students need to be proficient in the foundational skill of spelling teachers need to both help students learn the spelling of commonly used words and teach them how to generate and check plausible spellings of words they have not yet mastered as students are drafting they can learn how to apply spelling rules and check for correct spelling teachers can encourage very young children to write by allowing them to use invented spelling or trying to spell a word using what they know about letter sounds and patterns as students learn how to spell more words they should rely less on invented spelling while writing students can learn how to spell words by analogy they apply the spelling of a known word to generate a plausible spelling for an unknown but similar word for example if students know how to spell camp they can use that knowledge when learning to spell lamp

lesson_content.content:
Heading:

The Writing Rope: An Instructional Framework

Paragraph:

The Writing Rope (Sedita, 2019) is an instructional framework for teaching writing across all grades. It identifies five components of writing skills that students must learn in order to become skilled writers, represented by strands in a rope:

  • Critical Thinking: Generate ideas; gather information; apply the writing process.
  • Syntax: Write sophisticated, elaborated sentences.
  • Text Structure: Apply knowledge of paragraph and longer text structure; incorporate patterns of organization and related transitions.
  • Writing Craft: Consider the task, audience, and purpose; incorporate writing craft techniques when writing.
  • Transcription: Spell and handwrite or keyboard at an automatic level.

In order to be proficient writers, students must learn and integrate the skills, strategies, and techniques represented in all the strands, similar to the way strands are woven together to create a rope. View the graphic of The Writing Rope below. You can download a copy for future reference.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.2 Connections to Models of Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Title: Critical Thinking Strand Description:

This strand draws significantly on critical thinking and executive function skills, as well as the ability to develop background knowledge about a writing topic. Students engage in critical thinking as they think about what they want to communicate through their writing. If they are composing an informational or opinion piece, they may need to tap into comprehension skills to gather information from sources. 

 

This strand also includes awareness of the writing process (i.e., Think, Plan, Write, Revise). For the thinking and planning stages, students benefit from explicit instruction for brainstorming strategies and skills for gathering information from written and multi-media sources, such as note taking. They also need to learn planning strategies for organizing their thoughts, including the use of prewriting graphic organizers. Students need to be metacognitive and purposeful about working recursively through the stages of the writing process, and they benefit from explicit instruction in revising and editing strategies. 

 

Application to Primary Grades


Critical thinking for writing focuses on responding to text that is read or heard during read aloud. Writing is used to reinforce learning of anything that is read, said, or done in all subjects throughout the school day. It is also about young students making choices about what they want to say. 

 

The basic stages in the writing process can be introduced to young children and highlighted whenever students compose using drawing or words. 

 

This part of the rope is addressed in Module 3 The Writing Process and Module 8 Writing from Sources. 

Title: Syntax Strand Description:

Individual sentences communicate ideas that add up to make meaning. Efficient processing of sentence structure is necessary for listening and reading comprehension, as well as for communicating information and ideas in writing. Syntax is the study and understanding of grammar – the system and arrangement of words, phrases, and clauses that make up a sentence. Students develop syntactic awareness as they learn the correct use and relationship of words in sentences. This begins with exposure to standard English by listening to people talk, and reading or listening to written text. Students benefit from explicit instruction focused on building sentence skills, including activities such as sentence elaboration and sentence combining. 

 

Application to Primary Grades

 

Young children benefit from explicit instruction about basic sentence structure, including the parts of a sentence and how to expand their sentences through sentence elaboration practice. They also benefit from activities that develop syntactic awareness including sentence scrambles and sentence combining. 

 

This part of the rope is addressed specifically in Module 6 Syntax, Sentences. 

Title: Text Structure Strand Description:

Text structure is unique to written language, and awareness of text structure supports both writing and reading comprehension. Students benefit from explicit instruction for several levels of text structure. 

  • Narrative, informational, and opinion text structure: knowledge of the different organization structures for these three types of writing, including the use of introductions, body development, and conclusions 

  • Paragraph structure: understanding that written paragraphs are used to group text into manageable units that are organized around a main idea and supporting details

  • Patterns of organization: understanding that sentences and paragraphs can be organized to convey a specific purpose including description, sequence, cause and effect, compare and contrast, problem and solution 

  • Transition words or phrases: the use of words or phrases to link sentences, paragraphs or sections of text – including knowledge of transitions associated with specific patterns of organization 

Application to Primary Grades

 

The three main types of writing (informational, narrative, opinion) can be introduced in primary grades, including basic examples of introductions and conclusions. Initially, drawings with some words are used to represent these genres, leading to paragraph writing by the end of grade 2. 

 

Patterns of organization, along with simple examples of associated transitions, can also be introduced. 

 

Teachers should encourage students to read like a writer to see the reciprocal relationship of text structure between reading and writing. 

 

Module 4 Writer's Craft and Text Structure and Module 7 Paragraph Writing address these levels of text structure. 

Title: Writing Craft Strand Description:

This strand addresses skills and strategies often referred to as writers’ craft or writers’ moves. This includes a number of techniques that writers employ that affect writing style, text structure, and choice of words. Students benefit from explicit instruction in the following: 

  • Word choice: purposeful use of specific vocabulary, word placement and dialogue to convey meaning, and create an effect on the reader 

  • Writer’s voice: the techniques and style of writing an author uses to show emotion, personality, or point of view 

  • Literary devices: understanding and use of common literary elements (e.g., plot, setting, narrative, characters, theme) and literary techniques (e.g., imagery, personification, figurative language, alliteration, allegory, irony) 

When teachers share examples of mentor text that include clear examples of how authors use techniques, students are able to imitate them in their own writing.

 

This strand also addresses the importance of being mindful of the task, audience, and purpose when writing. Awareness of these elements influences decisions about word choice, tone, length, and style of a writing piece. 

Application to Primary Grades

 

The concept of writer’s craft can be introduced in the early grades, including basic techniques such as the use of illustrations and layout of visuals on the page, use of font type and size, organizing features such as headings and captions, and the use of dialogue and speech bubbles. 

 

The concept of the writer’s voice can also be introduced using simple examples found in read aloud books. 

 

This part of the rope is addressed in Module 4 Writer's Craft and Text Structure.

Title: Transcription Strand Description:

This strand addresses spelling and handwriting/keyboarding skills. They are basic skills that are needed to transcribe the words a writer wants to put into writing. Once students become automatic and fluent with spelling and handwriting/keyboarding, they can focus their attention on the other strands of the writing rope. If students do not develop fluency with these skills by the end of grade 3, this will put a constraint on writing development as they move into the later grades. 

 

Application to Primary Grades

 

Spelling and handwriting are best taught as part of phonics instruction. Keys to Early Writing focuses on oral and written composing, and therefore does not provide in-depth instructional suggestions for teaching spelling and handwriting. 

 

The role of transcription skills is addressed in Module 1 Writing Basics and Module 6 Syntax, Sentences.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.2 Connections to Models of Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

Writing is Complex!

Paragraph:

Proficient writers at all grade levels must integrate multiple skills, strategies, and techniques (summarized in the strands of The Writing Rope) in order to produce a quality piece of writing that reflects what they want to say.

To integrate all these strategies and skills, students must have sufficient executive function abilities including attention, the ability to set goals and organize, self-monitoring and regulation, and cognitive flexibility to be able to constantly adjust the application of multiple writing skills (Graham & Harris, 2000; Meltzer, 2010; Cartwright, 2015).

Heading:

Not-So Simple View of Writing

metadata.lesson_title: 1.2 Connections to Models of Writing Instruction
metadata.module_title: Connections to Models of Writing Instruction

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.2 Connections to Models of Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

The Not-So Simple View is a developmental model for writing that highlights several processes and skills that support writing (Berninger & Winn, 2006). In addition to transcription skills and composing (text generation), students must have sufficient executive function abilities to write. Working memory, the ability to hold onto ideas and information, is at the center of the model. Without working memory, writers cannot hold on to what they want to say long enough to be able to decide which words and sentences to write and how to spell those words.

Harris, Schmidt, and Graham explain the role of executive functions this way:

metadata.lesson_title: 1.2 Connections to Models of Writing Instruction
metadata.module_title: Connections to Models of Writing Instruction

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.2 Connections to Models of Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

While negotiating the rules and mechanics for writing, the writer must maintain focus on factors such as organization, form and features, purposes and goals, audience needs and perspectives, and evaluation of the communication between author and reader. Self-regulation of the writing process is critical; the writer must be goal oriented, resourceful, and reflective...For skilled authors, writing is a flexible, goal-directed activity, scaffolded by a rich source of cognitive processes and strategies for planning, text production, and revision.

metadata.lesson_title: 1.2 Connections to Models of Writing Instruction
metadata.module_title: Connections to Models of Writing Instruction

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.2 Connections to Models of Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Heading: Heading Paragraph:

Think about students you know who have attention and other related executive function difficulties. These students may only be able to plan for a few things to put in their drawings or sentences. They may have difficulty attending to spelling, handwriting, and composing their ideas at the same time. They may have difficulty maintaining their focus on their goals for drawing and writing, or sustaining attention for a writing task for long periods of time. These students benefit from scaffolds and explicit directions during writing instruction.

pdf_content.content:
--- Page 1 ---

Handwriting Instruction Checklist  
 
 
Checklist of Best Practices  
 
I teach children how to write each letter by:  
 
• Showing them how it is formed.  
• Describing how it is similar to and different from other letters.  
• Providing practice tracing, copying, and writing the letter from memory.  
• Keeping instructional sessions short, with frequent reviews and practice.  
• Asking them to identify or circle their best -formed letters.  
• Encouraging them to correct or rewrite poorly  formed letters.  
• Monitoring their practice to ensure that letters are formed correctly.  
• Reinforcing their successful efforts and providing corrective feedback as needed.  
 
I help children become more fluent in handwriting by:  
 
• Providing them with plenty of opportunities to write.  
• Eliminating interfering habits that may reduce handwriting fluency.  
• Having them copy a short passage several times, trying to write it a little faster each time.  
 
I promote handwriting development by:  
 
• Making sure that eac h child develops a comfortable and efficient pencil grip.  
• Encouraging children to sit in an upright position, leaning slightly forward, as they write.  
• Showing them how to place or position their paper when writing.  
• Teaching children to identify and name the letters of the alphabet.  
• Teaching them how to write both uppercase and lowercase letters.  
• Providing children with plenty of opportunities to use different types of writing instruments and paper.  
• Asking children to set goals for improving specific as pects of their handwriting.  
• Implementing appropriate procedures for left -handed writers, such as how to properly place or position 
their paper when writing.  
• Monitoring students’ handwriting, paying special attention to their instructional needs in letter 
formation, spacing, slant, a lignment, size, and line quality.  
• Dramatizing children’s progress in handwriting through the use of charts or graphs, praise, or posting 
neatly written papers.  
 
I assist students who are experiencing difficulty by:  
 
• Organizing my class so that I can prov ide additional handwriting instruction to children who need it.  
• Coordinating my handwriting instruction with the efforts of other professionals, such as an occupational 
therapist.  
• Placing special emphasis on teaching difficult letters, such as a, j, k, n,  q, u, and z.  
• Ensuring that they master one style of handwriting before a second style is introduced.  
• Considering if an alternative to handwriting, such as word processing or using a speech recognition 
program, is warranted.  
• Helping them develop positiv e attitudes about handwriting.  
• Talking with their parents about my handwriting program and soliciting advice.  
 
I make sure that I:  
 
• Encourage students to make all final drafts of papers neat and legible.  
• Maintain a balanced perspective on the role of handwriting in learning to write.  
 
 
Source: Graham, S. & Harris, K.R. (2002). Prevention and intervention for struggling writers. In M.R. Shinn, H.M. Walker, & G. Stoner  
(Eds.). Interventions for academic and behavior problems: Preventive and remedial approaches . Bethesda, MD: National Association o f 
School Psychologists.

• Teaching them how to write both uppercase and lowercase letters.  
• Providing children with plenty of opportunities to use different types of writing instruments and paper.  
• Asking children to set goals for improving specific as pects of their handwriting.  
• Implementing appropriate procedures for left -handed writers, such as how to properly place or position 
their paper when writing.  
• Monitoring students’ handwriting, paying special attention to their instructional needs in letter 
formation, spacing, slant, a lignment, size, and line quality.  
• Dramatizing children’s progress in handwriting through the use of charts or graphs, praise, or posting 
neatly written papers.  
 
I assist students who are experiencing difficulty by:  
 
• Organizing my class so that I can prov ide additional handwriting instruction to children who need it.  
• Coordinating my handwriting instruction with the efforts of other professionals, such as an occupational 
therapist.  
• Placing special emphasis on teaching difficult letters, such as a, j, k, n,  q, u, and z.  
• Ensuring that they master one style of handwriting before a second style is introduced.  
• Considering if an alternative to handwriting, such as word processing or using a speech recognition 
program, is warranted.  
• Helping them develop positiv e attitudes about handwriting.  
• Talking with their parents about my handwriting program and soliciting advice.  
 
I make sure that I:  
 
• Encourage students to make all final drafts of papers neat and legible.  
• Maintain a balanced perspective on the role of handwriting in learning to write.  
 
 
Source: Graham, S. & Harris, K.R. (2002). Prevention and intervention for struggling writers. In M.R. Shinn, H.M. Walker, & G. Stoner  
(Eds.). Interventions for academic and behavior problems: Preventive and remedial approaches . Bethesda, MD: National Association o f 
School Psychologists.

The National Council of Teachers of English (NCTE) notes the following about writing in the primary grades:

metadata.module_title: What Do We Know About K-2 Writing?

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.3 What Do We Know About K-2 Writing?
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

Young children possess knowledge about written language and a variety of forms of writing – stories, lists, signs – from an early age.

Paragraph:

The “language arts” develop in concert. Reading supports writing, drawing supports writing, and writing supports reading. Having opportunities to use multiple expressions of language increases language ability.

Paragraph:

Writing is a social activity and writing instruction should be embedded into social contexts. Through face-to-face interactions, children come to understand that writing serves many functions, that relationships exist between speaking and writing, and that writing is aimed at, and therefore must be sensitive to, an audience.

Paragraph:

Writing can be an effective tool for thinking and learning throughout the school day, in all subjects.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.3 What Do We Know About K-2 Writing?
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

"Writing is a fundamental part of engaging in professional, social, community, and civic activities. Because writing is a valuable tool for communication, learning, and self-expression, people who do not have adequate writing skills may be at a disadvantage and may face restricted opportunities for education and employment. Students should develop an early foundation in writing in order to communicate their ideas effectively and efficiently – yet many American students are not strong writers."

metadata.lesson_title: 1.3 What Do We Know About K-2 Writing?
metadata.module_title: What Do We Know About K-2 Writing?

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.3 What Do We Know About K-2 Writing?
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

Because writing is essential to success in school and beyond, writing instruction must be a key part of an elementary school literacy curriculum. For children in the early elementary grades, the goal should be developing basic skills to learn how to write, as well as to begin using writing to learn. 

It is sometimes assumed that writing is simply the reverse side of reading, and as young children learn to read, they will naturally learn to write. However, while reading and writing are complementary, students in kindergarten through grade two require dedicated time and explicit instruction to learn how to write. Through this instruction, young students will:

  •  learn how to use writing to communicate;

  •  learn and practice basic writing skills, strategies and techniques;

  •  and learn how to integrate multiple writing components to become skilled writers in later grades.

metadata.lesson_title: 1.3 What Do We Know About K-2 Writing?
metadata.module_title: What Do We Know About K-2 Writing?

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.3 What Do We Know About K-2 Writing?
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

Writing Development in the Primary Grades

Paragraph:

There is wide variation in writing ability from kindergarten through grade 2 and within a grade level!

There is a progression of developmental stages that most students typically follow as they move from kindergarten through grade 2. At first, young students may only be able to draw and compose orally, adding symbols that look like letters to their drawings. As they learn how print maps to speech, they begin to add labels, lists, phrases, and sentences to their drawings. Once they are able to write sentences, they can combine sentences into paragraphs and write different types of text for specific purposes. This includes learning the three main types of writing (genres) required in many state literacy standards: informational, narrative, and opinion.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.3 What Do We Know About K-2 Writing?
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

Emphasize Composing

Paragraph:

Teachers should let young students know that even though they may have difficulty spelling and writing words, what they have to say when they compose orally or in writing is important and valued. In the video below, Joan Sedita, author of Keys to Early Writing, explains why this is important, especially for struggling writers.

Figure  1-5 
 
Teaching  Elementary  Students  to be Effective  Writers:  A Practice  Guide 
(Graham et al., 2012)  
 
This report was published by the U.S. Department of Education’s Institute of Education 
Sciences.  The report  reviews  studies  related  to effective  practices  for teaching  writing  to students 
in kindergarten through grade five.  
Recommendations  
1. Provide  daily  time  for students  to write.  
2.Teach students tousethewriting process foravariety ofpurposes.  
2a Teach  students  the writing  process.  
1. Teach  students  strategies  for the various  components  of the writing  process.  
2. Gradually  release  writing  responsibility  from  the teacher  to the student.  
3. Guide  students  to select  and use appropriate  writing  strategies.  
4. Encourage  students  to be flexible  in their use of the components  of the writing 
process.  
 
2b Teach  students  to write  for a variety  of purposes.  
1. Help  students  understand  the different  purposes  of writing.  
2. Help  expand  students’  concept  of audience.  
3. Teach  students  to emulate  the features  of good  writing.  
4. Teach  students  techniques  for writing  effectively  for different  purposes.  
3. Teach  students  to become  fluent  with  handwriting,  spelling,  sentence  construction, 
typing, and word processing.  
1. Teach  very young  writers  how to hold a pencil  correctly  and form  letters  fluently 
and efficiently.  
2. Teach  students  to spell  words  correctly.  
3. Teach  students  to construct  sentences  for fluency,  meaning,  and style.  
4. Teach  students  to type fluently  and to use a word  processor  to compose.  
4. Create  an engaged  community  of writers.  
1. Teachers  should  participate  as members  of the community  by writing  and sharing 
their writing.  
2. Give  students  writing  choices.  
3. Encourage  students  to collaborate  as writers.  
4. Provide  students  with opportunities  to give and receive  feedback  throughout  the 
writing process.  
5. Publish  students’  writing  and extend  the community  beyond  the classroom.

pdf_content.content:
--- Page 1 ---

© 2020  Keys  to Literacy   Figure  1-9 
 
EXPECTATIONS  
Composing  Skills  EXPECTATIONS  
Transcription  Skills  
Kindergarten  
• Composing  is expressed  primarily  through 
speaking, dictating, or drawing.  
• Students  may attempt  to write  messages  of 
their own and read them back.  
• Students  produce  complete  sentences 
when speaking.  
• Students  use frequently  occurring  nouns, 
verbs, and question words while 
composing.  
• Students  capitalize  the first word  when 
writing a sentence.  Students:  
• Know  that writing  differs  from  drawing, 
has distinct  letter  forms,  and can be read 
back 
• Know  how to write  at least some  letter 
forms  
• Know  that spaces  are between  words  
• Know that writing goes from left to right 
and is organized  from  top to bottom  of the 
page 
• May or may not know  the alphabetic 
principle  and produce  early  phonetic 
spellings of some sounds in words  
 
Grade  1 
• Composing  is expressed  primarily  through 
speaking, drawing, words, and gradually 
written sentences.  
• Students  begin  to produce  the four types  of 
sentences (declarative, interrogative, 
imperative, exclamatory) and compound 
sentences when speaking.  
• Students use a wider variety of nouns, 
pronouns,  conjunctions,  prepositions,  and verb 
tenses while composing.  
• Students may be amenable to adding details to 
drawings  and text in response  to feedback  from 
peers and adults.  Students:  
• Know the alphabetic principle and 
spell  by sound  using  alphabet  letter 
names and sound -symbol 
correspondences  
• Remember  the spellings  of commonly 
used high frequency words  
• Begin  to write  words  in sequence  with 
word spaces, capitalize the first word, 
and use punctuation at the ends of 
sentences  
 
Grade  2 
• Composing  is expressed  primarily  through 
speaking and writing sentences.  
• Students write compound and complex 
sentences,  and all four types  of sentences.  
• Students  begin  to link sentences  into a basic 
paragraph.  
• There  is more  independence  in generating 
ideas.  • Spelling and handwriting are 
becoming more sufficient to focus 
more  on the demands  of composing.  
• Students apply basic capitalization. 
rules and begin using more 
punctuation (e.g., commas in letter 
greetings,  apostrophe  for contraction  
and possessives).

--- Page 2 ---

© 2020  Keys  to Literacy   • Students  use more  complicated  forms  of nouns 
and pronouns, irregular verbs, and a variety of 
adjectives and adverbs when composing.  
• Students  begin  to be aware  of task, audience, 
purpose.  
• Students may be amenable to adding details 
and text revision  in response  to feedback  from 
peers and adults.   
 
Grade  3 
• Composing  is expressed  primarily  through 
writing.  
• Students  write  compound  and complex 
sentences.  
• Students  link sentences  into multi -paragraphs.  
• Students use a wider range of vocabulary, 
including  terms  that represent  abstract  concepts 
when composing.  
• Students  write  with an awareness  of task, 
audience, purpose.  
• Students  revise  writing  with feedback  support 
from peers and adults.  • Spelling and handwriting are 
sufficiently  fluent  to focus  on the 
demands of composing.  
• Students use more complex 
punctuation  (e.g.,  commas  and 
quotation marks in dialogue).

Transcript: Welcome to the overview on teaching foundational skills to help students become effective writers students need to learn foundational writing skills so that they can focus more on developing and communicating ideas in their writing handwriting spelling sentence construction typing and word processing should become effortless as students move to middle and upper elementary school younger students need explicit instruction and frequent practice to acquire and Polish these skills young writers in kindergarten and 1st grade need to learn how to hold a pencil and form letters correctly teachers should demonstrate how to hold a pencil comfortably between the thumb and forefinger while resting on the middle finger although children may change their grip overtime a comfortable pencil grip will prevent students from getting tired which can discourage writing teachers need to show young children how to form print and cursive letters correctly from memory handwriting practice diagrams can be helpful in showing students how to form letters practicing specific letters in isolation is not sufficient to achieve handwriting proficiency students need to apply their handwriting skills to authentic writing activities handwriting practice is most effective when done in multiple short sessions students need to be proficient in the foundational skill of spelling teachers need to both help students learn the spelling of commonly used words and teach them how to generate and check plausible spellings of words they have not yet mastered as students are drafting they can learn how to apply spelling rules and check for correct spelling teachers can encourage very young children to write by allowing them to use invented spelling or trying to spell a word using what they know about letter sounds and patterns as students learn how to spell more words they should rely less on invented spelling while writing students can learn how to spell words by analogy they apply the spelling of a known word to generate a plausible spelling for an unknown but similar word for example if students know how to spell camp they can use that knowledge when learning to spell lamp

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

Researchers and practitioners have been studying writing instruction for many decades to identify effective instructional practices. Why is it important for teachers to become familiar with this research?

Teachers who deliver high quality instruction combine what is sometimes referred to as the art of teaching (how they teach in an engaging way) with the science of teaching (what research tells us is most effective instruction). It is essential for teachers to be sure their instruction is supported by research - this is often referred to as evidence-based instruction.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Heading: Heading Paragraph:

While the goal for this Keys to Early Writing professional development course is to provide practical teaching suggestions, references and connections to research findings that support these suggestions are embedded throughout the course. You are encouraged to become familiar with writing research so you can explain to colleagues and administrators why you are using what you learn in the course or to support practices you already use.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

General Writing Research Findings

Paragraph:

There are several teaching practices that come up as effective across numerous studies, listed below.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

  • Teach students that there are different purposes for writing.

  • Assign writing tasks for real audiences that are relevant to students’ everyday lives.

  • Teach the stages of the writing process.

  • Explicitly teach writing skills, strategies, and techniques.

  • Provide procedural scaffolds.

Paragraph:

  • Provide opportunities for students to collaborate with peers and the teacher.

  • Use mentor models for students to emulate.

  • Provide feedback.

  • Increase the amount students write and time spent on writing instruction.

  • Create a supportive, engaging writing environment.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

Sources: Hidi & Boscolo, 2006; Chapman, 2006; Donovan & Smolkin, 2006; Boscolo & Gelati, 2007; Coker, 2007; MacArthur, 2007; Pritchard & Honeycutt, 2007

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

Writing Research Findings Related to Early Writing Instruction

Paragraph:

There are two research sources based on meta-analyses of research on effective elementary writing instruction that are referenced throughout the course. The first is Teaching Elementary School Students to Be Effective Writers (Graham et al., 2012) that offers four main recommendations:

  1. Provide daily time for students to write. 
  2. Teach students to use the writing process for a variety of purposes.
  3. Teach students to become fluent with handwriting, spelling, sentence construction, typing, and word processing. 
  4. Create an engaged community of writers. 

Review the details for these recommendations in the document below. You can download a copy for your records. Watch the video that summarizes the recommendations, and then view the infographic after the video (Institute of Education Sciences).

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

Research Meta-Analysis Article 

Paragraph:

The second research source based on a meta-analysis of research related to grades kindergarten through grade 8 is the article Research-Based Writing Practices and the Common Core: Meta-Analysis and Meta-Synthesis (Graham, Harris, Santangelo, 2015).  Review the article's 13 recommendations (with explanations for each recommendation) in the document below. When you are done, select the boxes next to any practices in the list below that you use on a regular basis. Be sure to keep these evidence-based practices in mind as you teach writing!

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.4 Research About Effective Writing Instruction
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

1. Create a writing environment that is positive and supportive.

Paragraph:

2. Establish writing routines that create a pleasant and motivating writing environment.

Paragraph:

3. Implement a process approach to writing.

Paragraph:

4. Create routines that ensure students write frequently.

Paragraph:

5. Design instructional routines where students compose together.

Paragraph:

6. Establish goals for students' writing.

Paragraph:

7. Use twenty-first-century writing tools.

Paragraph:

8. Provide feedback.

Paragraph:

9. Ensure students acquire needed writing skills, knowledge, and strategies.

Paragraph:

10. Teach foundational writing skills.

Paragraph:

11. Increase students' knowledge about writing.

Paragraph:

12. Teach students strategies for planning, drafting, revising, and editing.

Paragraph:

13. Use writing as a tool to support students' learning.

lesson_content.content:

pdf_content.content:
--- Page 1 ---

Figure 1.6 
Research -Based  Writing  Practices  and the Common  Core:  
Meta -analysis and Meta -synthesis 
(Graham,  Harris,  Santangelo,  2015)  
 
This article summarizes research -supported practices that can be used to meet Common Core 
State  Standards  writing  objectives  in kindergarten  to grade  eight.  These  practices  were  identified 
by conducting a meta -analysis of writing intervention studies and the practices of exceptional 
literacy teachers.  
 
Recommendations  
1. Create  a Writing  Environment  That  is Positive  and Supportive  
 
Given  the demands  of state  writing  standards,  it is important  to develop  a writing  environment 
that is motivating, pleasant, and nonthreatening, where teachers support students and their 
writing efforts, and students support each other.  
 
2. Establish  Writing  Routines  That  Create  a Pleasant  and Motivating  Writing  Environment  
 
It is helpful  to establish  routines  and create  a positive  classroom  where  students  share  their  work 
with others, are encouraged to try hard, and are motivated to write.  
 
3. Implement  a Process  Approach  to Writing  
 
A process approach to writing includes creating a pleasant and positive writing environment, 
promoting high levels of student interactions to support writing, writing for real audiences and 
authentic purposes, stressing personal responsibility and ownership of writing projects, and 
encouraging  self-reflection  and evaluation.  This type  of model  was found  to have  a positive  effect 
on the quality of writing produced by students in grades 1 to 8.  
4. Create  Routines  That  Ensure  Students  Write  Frequently  
 
The basic premise is that students must write frequently if they are to grow as writers. Research 
finds that when teachers increased how frequently students in grades 2 -8 wrote, there was a 
corresponding  improvement  in the quality  of their  writing.  It also finds  that for students  in grades 
2-6, extra writing made students better readers.  
 
5. Design  Instructional  Routines  Where  Students  Compose  Together  
 
Having students work together when writing is an effective practice. A common characteristic of 
studies  that tested  collaborative  writing  was that students  were  provided  with  specific  directions 
for what to do when working together and then taught how to carry out these guidelines.

--- Page 2 ---

6. Establish  Goals  for Students’  Writing  
 
Research  finds  that writing  quality  is enhanced  when  teachers  set high,  but realistic  expectations 
for students’ writing. This includes setting clear, specific, and reasonably challenging goals for 
student writing.  
 
7. Use Twenty -First -Century  Writing  Tools  
 
Word processors have a number of advantages over writing by hand, as electronic text is legible, 
and it can easily  be deleted,  added,  rewritten,  or moved.  Research  finds  that the quality  of writing 
of students in grades 1 to 8 who used word processing versus writing by hand improved.  
 
8. Provide  Feedback  
 
Research finds that the use of formative assessment, (teacher, peer, and self -assessment) 
improved children’s writing. When teachers monitor students’ progress as writers they can  
adjust  classroom  practices  to meet  the collective  as well as the individual needs  of their  students.  
 
9. Ensure  Students  Acquire  Needed  Writing  Skills,  Knowledge,  and Strategies  
 
Research supports teaching writing skills (e.g., handwriting, spelling, grammar and usage), 
attributes  of specific  types  of text (e.g.,  opinion,  informative,  narrative)  and writing  strategies  that 
support planning, revising and editing.  
 
10. Teach  Foundational  Writing  Skills  
 
Research finds that teaching spelling and handwriting to grade 1 to 3 students enhanced the 
overall  quality  of their  writing.  It also finds  that teaching  sentence  construction  through  sentence - 
combining improves the quality of student writing.  
11. Increase  Students’  Knowledge  About  Writing  
 
Three  types  of knowledge  that are important  are knowledge  about  the writing  topic,  knowledge 
about genre, and knowledge about the words used to convey meaning. Research supports the 
following:  
• Have students gather ideas and information to write about through prewriting activities 
such  as locating  writing  content  through  brainstorming,  reading,  drawing,  and/or  by using 
graphic organizers.  
• Teach  students  the basic  elements  of the different  types  of text (text  structure).  
• Provide  students  with  good  models  of written  text.  
• Teach  students  vocabulary  that will improve  their  text.  
 
12. Teach  Students  Strategies  for Planning,  Drafting,  Revising,  and Editing

--- Page 3 ---

Research  finds  that it is beneficial  for teachers  to encourage  students  to employ  a variety  of 
planning, revising, and editing strategies to help them create and improve what they write. 
Research also supports the explicit teaching of strategies.  
 
13. Use Writing  as a Tool  to Support  Students’  Learning  
 
Research  supports  having  students  use writing  as a tool for learning,  including  writing  about 
content presented in class and writing about materials read. This results in improved 
comprehension of such material. Writing to learn activities include writing short answers to 
questions, taking notes, writing a summary, and writing more extended responses.

transcript_content.content: Let's talk about handwriting and Oregon keyboarding if you want to get something out on paper you have to be able to write it you also have to be able to spell it but even if you know how to spell the word you have to be able to get those letters down on the paper and if students of any age have to put too much energy into thinking about how to form those letters it's going to take energy away from the critical thinking parts of being able to write now there's a lot of research out there that shows that students by the time they're in 4th grade if they're not fluent with that handwriting and spending too much it really effects the quality of their writing of their composing now there's uncertainty there's no set answer to the question about should it be cursive should it be manuscript but we do know that whichever it is students have to become fluent in that now let's also make the connection to keyboarding or typing very young children don't have the they're not developmentally able to do the kind of you know reporting that adults and older students can but they can hunt and Peck once students hit about 5th or 6th grade they're developmentally more able to do the keyboarding and there's great debate over whether we should spend time with handwriting or go right to keyboarding you're not here to answer that question I do believe that for elementary children in particular there's also so much research that shows that if students are learning to handwrite the letters at the same time that they're learning the letter sound correspondences that improves the letter writing and it also improves the alphabetic knowledge that they're developping during a phonics lesson so for lots of reasons the ability to be fluent in that transcription skill of handwriting and or keyboarding is absolutely essential

Lesson Title: 1.5 Teaching Principles
Description: 
Type: blocks

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

Teaching Principles

Paragraph:

There are several teaching principles that teachers should keep in mind as they teach writing in the early grades. As you review the chart below, note which items you already use while teaching writing and identify at least one item you would like to use more often. You can download a copy of the chart below.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

Gradual Release of Responsibility

This model of instruction is also referred to as an I do it, We do it, You do it approach to teaching. It includes explicit instruction using a direct approach to introduce and teach a new writing skill, strategy, or technique (Pearson & Gallagher, 1983). Explicit instruction includes explaining a skill and modeling how it is applied using think-aloud, and providing guided practice with feedback. 

Systematic  Instruction of Writing Strategies

Systematic instruction refers to a planned and logical sequence of teaching, proceeding in small steps and checking for student understanding before moving to the next step. In the early grades, this includes a scope and sequence that moves from drawing, to labeling and lists, to sentences, to paragraphs. 

Differentiated Instruction

Differentiated instruction calls for designing instruction to suit individual student needs rather than using a standardized approach to instruction that assumes all students learn to write the same way. 

Scaffolding

Scaffolding is assistance offered by a teacher or a peer to support learning a writing skill that a student is initially unable to grasp independently, and then removal of the assistance once the skill is learned. This is not the same as doing the work for the student. Scaffolding is a hallmark of differentiated instruction. 

Use Multi-Sensory Activities

Teach writing skills by including activities that incorporate auditory, visual, tactile, and kinesthetic pathways for learning. 

Automaticity Through Practice

When students learn a writing skill at an automatic (mastery) level, they have learned it so thoroughly that they can use it with little or no conscious attention. In particular, teachers should strive to develop automatic spelling and handwriting skills so students can focus their attention on the composing aspects of writing. Significant guided practice is an essential element of developing automaticity.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

More About the Gradual Release of Responsibility

Paragraph:

The teacher is the centerpiece of the I do it stage, employing an explicit and systematic approach that includes modeling and think aloud to introduce and teach a writing skill. At the We do it stage, students practice the skill in whole group or small groups. The teacher guides this practice and provides targeted, corrective feedback to students. During this stage, the teacher differentiates instructional support and provides scaffolds based on the individual needs of students. At the You do it stage, students are able to use the skill independently. The amount of guided practice that students need to reach the independent level varies from student to student. Listen to Joan Sedita, author of Keys to Early Writing, explain this model.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
lesson_content.content:
Heading: Heading Paragraph:

The steps below gradually release responsibility for learning and using a writing skill, strategy, or technique to students:

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

Identify, define, explain the writing skill. Explicitly teach the skill using modeling and think aloud.

Paragraph:

Show the skill in model writing samples. Show students writing samples that incorporate the skill and help them analyze how the author applied the skill.

Paragraph:

Practice skill-building activities. Provide opportunities for students to practice the skill.

Paragraph:

Modify and revise writing samples. Have students practice applying the skill to writing samples that are partially completed or that need “fixing”. The samples can be generated by the teacher, or they can be anonymous student samples. Have students work collaboratively to apply the skill to improve the samples.

Paragraph:

Use the skill in a collaborative writing piece. Provide opportunities for students to work collaboratively to complete a writing task that incorporates the skill. Give feedback about the use of the skill, and find opportunities for students to revise their pieces using this feedback.

Paragraph:

Students use the skill independently. When students are ready, have them independently complete a writing piece that incorporates the writing skill. Continue to provide feedback and opportunities for revision.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

Mentor Text

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
lesson_content.content:
Paragraph:

Most people learn to write by emulating others, similar to learning other new skills such as playing a sport or a musical instrument. Writing models, also known as mentor text, are used to demonstrate strong writing for students so they can imitate style, language, and structure in their own writing.  Joan Sedita, author of Keys to Early Writing, explains mentor text in the video below.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
lesson_content.content:
Heading: Heading Paragraph:

Mentor models can be used to show how authors use writing strategies and techniques, also called writing moves. Portalupi and Fletcher (2001) explain that students need to “apprentice themselves” to good writers as they learn to write. Mentor text may be used broadly to teach students the difference between opinion, informational, and narrative text. It can also be used more specifically to teach students specific writing techniques, such as how to use shapes to draw, how to write an expanded sentence, how to write a topic sentence for a paragraph, or how to use transition words and phrases such as finally, in addition, another. 

You will learn more about using mentor text in Module 4 Writer's Craft and Text Structure. A list of examples of mentor text that can be used to teach techniques and text structures shared in this course can be found at the end of Module 9 Wrap Up.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

Knowledge Check

Paragraph:

When we show up to the present moment with all of our senses, we invite the world to fill us with joy. The pains of the past are behind us. The future has yet to unfold. But the now is full of beauty simply waiting for our attention.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.5 Teaching Principles

Teaching Principles: Kindergarten to Grade 2 Wri7ng 
Gradual Release of This model of instruction is also referred to as an I do it, We do it, You do 
Responsibility it approach to teaching. It includes explicit instruction using a direct 
approach to introduce and teach a new writing skill, strategy, or 
technique (Pearson & Gallagher, 1983). Explicit instruction includes explaining a skill and modeling how it is applied using think-aloud, and 
providing guided practice with feedback. 
Systematic Systematic instruction refers to a planned and logical sequence of 
Instruction teaching, proceeding in small steps and checking for student understanding before moving to the next step. In the early grades, this includes a scope and sequence that moves from drawing, t o labeling and 
lists, to sentences, to paragraphs . 
Differentiated 
Instruction Differentiated instruction calls for designing instruction to suit individual student needs rather than using a standardized approach to instruction that assumes all students will learn to write the same way. 
Scaffolding Scaffolding is assistance offered by a teacher or a peer to support learning a writing skill that a student is initially unable to grasp independently, and then removing the assistance once the skill is learned. This is not the same as doing the work for t he student. 
Scaffolding is a hallmark of differentiated instruction. 
Use Multi -Sensory 
Activities Teach writing skills by including activities that incorporate auditory, visual, tactile, and kinesthetic pathways for learning. 
Automaticity When students learn a writing skill at an automatic (mastery) level, they 
Through Practice have learned it so thoroughly that they can use it with little or no conscious attention. In particular, teachers should strive to develop automatic spelling and handwriting skills s o students can focus their 
attention on the composing aspects of writing. Significant guided practice is an essential element of developing automaticity.

lesson_content.title: Content: 1.6 Composing vs. Transcription Skills
lesson_content.content:
Heading:

Composing Versus Transcription Skills

Paragraph:

Spelling and handwriting are transcription skills that young students need to transcribe the words they want to put into writing. If spelling and handwriting are not fluent and require considerable effort, students cannot focus on higher level composing skills. One of the recommendations in the research guide Teaching Elementary School Students to Be Effective Writers (Graham et al., 2012) is to teach students how to become fluent with these transcription skills.

View the kindergarten through grade three details about grade-level expectations for composing and transcriptions skills in the document below. 

Next, watch the video about the importance of transcription skills (Institute of Education Sciences).

Transcript: For students who struggle with writing and they could be older students who are still having difficulty writing sentences they could be younger students who are are having a difficult time just getting two or three words on the page one of the things we have to remember that the ultimate goal of writing is to communicate right it's it's to get out to somebody else what you have to say what we have to remember though is that composing can happen orally it doesn't have to be done through writing right or through typing you know I think about one of my sons who had a very difficult time with writing with spelling and yet he had so much to say and so whether it's an older student or whether it's a really young one what you have to get them to realize is it's what they have to say that's important and that you value that so if you can find an opportunity to have a young child who can't get the words down if you can have them dictate to you to say what do you want to say about this or even do a little bit of a drawing and then use that as a jumping off point to help them remember what they want to compose and then fill in the words for them now you're helping them realize that yes you're frustrated getting the words out on the page or spelling them but you know what that doesn't mean you're not a good writer being a good writer isn't just about spelling or about handwriting so I think that's that's how we encourage young ones to not get discouraged when they don't have the transcription skills to get out on paper all the great ideas that they have they may also develop really good sentence ability and text structure but its structure that they're able to express verbally if not on paper
